actions.thought: T9s is a strong suited connector and a premium hand to open with from the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 3x the big blind to take the initiative and build the pot with a hand that plays very well post-flop.

I am dealt a very weak offsuit hand, 9h 6d, in the Small Blind. The Button has opened to 3x the big blind. Calling or raising with such a weak hand out of position is a losing play. The standard and optimal choice here is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (7-3 offsuit). Even though the price to call is relatively cheap, playing such a weak hand out of position against a button open is mathematically negative expectation. Folding is the correct discipline to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

With 7c9c on the Button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raise. I have a suited one-gapper that plays well post-flop and gives me the initiative, plus I'll have position on the blinds. A standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here.

I hold a pair of eights in the small blind against a button open. This is a strong hand that plays well in 3-bet pots, allowing me to take the initiative and isolate the opponent. By raising to 800, I can define my range, build value, and potentially take the pot down pre-flop.

I am holding Jack-Trey of hearts in the Big Blind. The Button opened with a raise to 250, and the Small Blind three-bet to 800. J3s is a weak suited hand that is significantly behind the ranges of a standard button open and a small blind three-bet. Calling 700 more to see a flop out of position against two opponents, one of whom has shown significant strength, is not a profitable play. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I opened with a speculative suited one-gapper (7c9c) from the Button, but faced a large 3-bet from the Small Blind (to 8bb). Although I have position, calling a 3-bet of this size with 79s is generally unprofitable. The hand doesn't have enough equity against a typical SB 3-betting range and will frequently miss the flop or hit marginal hands that are tough to play against continued aggression. The best move is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no prior action, there is no strategic merit in playing this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 3c. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in the range. The action folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to contend with. While I could attempt to steal or complete the blind, playing 23s out of position against a competent opponent is generally a losing strategy. At this early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than bloating a pot with a bottom-tier hand.

I am on the button with 6c 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have positional advantage, 6-4 offsuit does not play well post-flop and is not worth a raise or a limp in this spot. I will fold and wait for a better hand.
Kh8h is a strong hand blind versus blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and allows us to take the pot down pre-flop or build a pot with a suited king.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce suited, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a small blind raise. The pot odds are favorable to call, as I only need to contribute 200 more to a 400 pot, and being in position post-flop is not the case, but I have equity and potential to hit a flush or top pair.

On a dry, paired board like QQ4, I have a strong range advantage since I raised pre-flop. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot will fold out a lot of the Big Blind's missed overcards (like Ax or better Kx) and underpairs, while risking the minimum when they do happen to have a Queen or pocket pair. Our hand also has some equity with an overcard and a backdoor flush draw.

I hold the nut flush draw on a paired board with an Ace high. The opponent bet 200 into a 600 pot, providing excellent pot odds of 5:1. Calling is the mathematically optimal play to realize my equity and see the turn card, as I have significant outs to improve to the nut flush.

On this turn, the 2 of diamonds changes very little. Our opponent called the flop, meaning they likely have a pocket pair, a Queen, a 4, or at least Ace high. Since we only have King-high with no draw, firing a second barrel is unlikely to generate enough fold equity against hands that beat us, and we have very little equity if called. Checking is the safest and most standard play. If the opponent bets, we can comfortably fold.

I am in position on the turn with a nut flush draw and a pair of deuces. The opponent checked, which indicates potential weakness or a trap on a paired board. Checking back is the optimal play as it allows me to take a free card to realize my equity for the river, avoids bloating the pot against a potential Queen, and keeps my range balanced.

On the river, the Ten completes some floats like JT or T9, and our King high has very little showdown value. Our opponent's flop call and turn check indicates a marginal made hand like a low pocket pair, an Ace-high float, or a hand that just paired the Ten. A bluff here would need to successfully fold out Ace-highs and small pocket pairs, but since the river card connects with a portion of their floating range, it's safer to give up and check. I will fold to any bet.

I am holding a pair of deuces on a board of Qh Qd 4c 2d Th. The opponent checked to me on the river after betting the flop and checking the turn, which suggests they either have a weak hand, a missed draw, or are potentially trapping. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as my pair of deuces is quite weak, and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff against a range that might have a queen or a better pocket pair. Checking back is the optimal way to take my showdown value and avoid running into a check-raise.
Q8 offsuit is a marginal holding even 3-handed on the button. Since I don't have a strong read that the blinds are over-folding, I will fold and wait for a better hand to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand (2h 3c). While I have already invested 50 chips, there is no value in playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve stack and avoid playing a negative expected value hand.

I am holding 6s 5d, which is a very weak hand with low equity. Given that I am on the button and it is early in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips with such a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 4c, which is a very weak hand. The Button has already folded, leaving only the Big Blind to beat. While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, 94o has poor playability and very low equity if called by a strong opponent like 'Pro'. Given that we are early in the tournament (hand 6 of 150) and have plenty of chips, there is no reason to defend or attack with such a marginal hand. I will choose the conservative route and fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight offsuit. In a three-handed game, this hand is marginal for an opening raise. Given that both Pro and Flashlight have shown some aggression in previous hands (Pro raising pre-flop and Flashlight 3-betting), I prefer to play conservatively early in the tournament. Folding here avoids potentially difficult post-flop spots with a weak top-pair or middle-pair hand.
With 93o in the Small Blind, the hand is far too weak to play even in a blind vs blind scenario. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct play to preserve my stack.
Q7 offsuit is too weak of a hand to open-raise, even from the Button in a 3-handed game. It's better to fold and wait for a more playable hand or a better spot to attack the blinds.

Holding 3h 2s, which is essentially the worst possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em, there is no strategic justification to complete the blind or raise. Even though the call is cheap, playing such a weak hand out of position against the big blind is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined and professional decision.

I am holding 2s-3c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no equity and a high likelihood of being dominated, playing this hand from the button is strategically unsound. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With the button folding, I am in a Blind vs Blind situation. 98o is a decent hand to open from the Small Blind to exert pressure on the Big Blind. Raising allows me to take down the blinds immediately or play the hand with initiative if called. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate in this spot.

I am facing a raise from the Small Blind while holding 54o in the Big Blind. This is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand with poor equity and post-flop playability. Calling or raising here would be unprofitable, so the standard play is to fold.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with King-Trey offsuit. While K3o is a weak hand, position is paramount in short-handed play. I have had success stealing the blinds with a 3x open in previous hands, and I want to maintain an aggressive image. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds; if I am called, I will have the advantage of acting last on all subsequent streets.

Facing a button raise, A5o is too weak to call from the small blind as it plays poorly post-flop out of position. It can occasionally be used as a 3-bet bluff, but a fold is the standard and lower-variance approach against an active button.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 5c against a button raise. The pot odds are favorable to call, as I need to put in 200 to win a total pot of 650. Suited connectors have decent playability post-flop, making a call the most mathematically sound and balanced action here.

The flop of Kc-Qh-Jh is highly coordinated and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which typically consists of high cards. With 6c-5c, I have completely missed the board and hold no significant equity (no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw). Betting would be an ill-advised bluff into a strong range, and calling a bet would be mathematically incorrect. Checking is the optimal strategic choice to evaluate the opponent's action and control the size of the pot.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Kings, 3) on a very coordinated board (Kc-Qh-Jh). While I have the best hand at the moment against many parts of Flashlight's range, this board texture hits a Big Blind calling range very hard, containing many straight draws, two-pair combinations, and better Kings. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid getting check-raised off my hand, and can evaluate the turn card. If the turn is relatively safe, I can still extract value or call a reasonably sized bet.

The turn card (4d) is a brick that does not improve my hand. I have 6-high with no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Since I checked the flop and the opponent checked behind, the pot remains small. Betting here would be a pure bluff with low chance of success, as the board is very coordinated and likely hit the opponent's range. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see the river cheaply.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a very wet board (Kc-Qh-Jh-4d). Flashlight has checked twice, which could indicate weakness or a trap. By checking back the turn, I continue to control the pot size and guarantee seeing the river. My hand has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for three streets of value, especially since any King with a better kicker, two-pair, or a straight already beats me. Checking back keeps the pot manageable and prevents me from being blown off the hand by a check-raise.

I hold 6c 5c on a board of Kc-Qh-Jh-4d-9s. I have high card six and no showdown value. Both I and my opponent have checked down the flop and turn. Betting now would be a pure bluff against a range that likely includes many made hands (pairs or better) that would call any bet, or hands that have already checked down to realize showdown value. Given that my hand is effectively air, checking to see the showdown is the only logical path as I cannot win a showdown unless my opponent is also bluffing or folding, but checking allows me to lose the minimum while avoiding a potential raise.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board that is now very coordinated (Kc-Qh-Jh-4d-9s). The 9s on the river completes a straight for any Ten (T-x). Flashlight has checked for the third time, suggesting they either have a weak hand, a missed draw, or are potentially trapping with a straight or two-pair. While my King has showdown value, betting for value is extremely thin here because most hands that call me will likely have me beaten (better Kings, two pairs, or straights), and I would be forced to fold to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to get to showdown for free and likely win against missed heart draws or lower pairs like Queens or Jacks.

Ten-four offsuit is a very weak hand with low equity and poor playability post-flop. Even though we are on the button, there is no reason to put chips in the pot or attempt a steal with such a weak holding against the blinds. Folding is the optimal play.

Holding 3c 8h in the small blind is a very weak hand. Even with the button folding, playing this out of position against the big blind is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play here to conserve my stack.

My hole cards are 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. With no action in front of me on the button, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for stronger opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit (T3o) after the button folded. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind. While I could attempt to steal or limp, T3o is near the bottom of the starting hand range and doesn't play well post-flop. I will choose to fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable hand.

I am on the button with pocket Jacks, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to raise for value and potentially take down the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. A standard 3x raise to 300 chips is consistent with previous betting patterns in this game and allows me to build the pot effectively.

Holding 9d 7h offsuit in the Small Blind facing a button raise to 3 BBs. This hand is too weak to play from out of position, and the gap between the cards makes it play poorly post-flop. A clear fold.

With Qs5s in the Big Blind, I am receiving reasonable pot odds to call a button open. While Q5s is not a premium hand, it is suited and holds blockers to some Broadway hands. Folding is too passive given the odds, and 3-betting is unnecessary aggression with this specific holding. Calling to see a flop is the standard, balanced play.

I am out of position on an Ace-high board that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. With Queen-high and no made pair, I have very little equity to continue against a standard continuation bet. Checking is the mandatory play here to see how the opponent proceeds.

I have pocket Jacks on an Ace-high flop after being the pre-flop aggressor. While I could bet to represent the Ace, checking back is a strong pot-control move. If my opponent has an Ace, I am significantly behind, and if they have a draw or a lower pair, I can often induce a bluff on later streets or get to showdown more cheaply. Checking also protects my range by not always betting when I miss an Ace. I will check to see the turn for free.

With Queen-high on a board of A-6-7-9, my hand lacks sufficient equity to bet for value or as a semi-bluff. The opponent checked behind on the flop, which might indicate a weak range or a hesitant play, but I cannot exploit this with a bluff given the connected nature of the board. Checking is the standard play to preserve my stack, keep the pot small, and potentially reach a showdown if checked around again or to fold to a significant bet on the river.

I checked the flop for pot control because of the Ace on the board. The turn 9h doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it adds some straight possibilities. Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting they are unlikely to have a strong Ace. While my pocket Jacks are likely ahead of many hands in Flashlight's range, checking back again ensures I get to a showdown cheaply and prevents being blown off the hand by a check-raise. It also avoids building a large pot where I'm uncertain of my standing. I will check to see the river for free.

I am in the big blind with Queen-high, which has no showdown value against the opponent's range on an Ace-high board. Since the opponent checked behind on both the flop and the turn, their range is capped but likely still contains hands that beat Queen-high, such as any pair or even worse high cards that might have hit the board. Betting would turn my hand into a pure bluff, which is unlikely to succeed against a cautious opponent. Checking is the standard, safest play to reach a showdown at no cost.

I have pocket Jacks on a board with an overcard (Ace). Since my opponent has checked through every street (flop, turn, and river), it is highly likely they have a marginal hand or a missed draw. While I could bet for value, checking back ensures I get to a showdown and win the pot against hands like middle pairs or missed draws, while avoiding the risk of a check-raise or losing to a slow-played Ace.
76o is a weak, unsuited hand with low equity. Even on the button, it is too weak to open-raise, so folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the small blind with a pocket pair of deuces, facing only the big blind after the button folded. Pocket deuces have decent equity heads-up, and taking the initiative by raising is strategically superior to just calling, as it allows me to potentially win the pot pre-flop or control the betting going forward.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 4c. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot of 400, I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring only 25% equity to continue. In a 3-handed game, a Small Blind opening range is typically very wide. Qc 4c is a suited hand that performs reasonably well against wide ranges and has decent post-flop playability, even though I will be out of position. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board. While this is a standard spot to C-bet as the range advantage often lies with the raiser, holding pocket deuces means I am effectively bluffing if I bet, as I have no showdown value against any hand containing an Ace or better. The opponent's calling range from the Big Blind is wide and significantly impacts this texture, making an Ace very likely to be in their hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and avoid inflating a pot where I am likely behind, while keeping my range disguised.

On a board of Ah 5c 3h, I have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 2 for the wheel). Flashlight, who raised pre-flop from the Small Blind, has checked to me. While I could represent an Ace or a heart draw by betting, checking back is a safer play that allows me to realize my equity for free. Flashlight might be checking a marginal hand or an Ace to trap, and with limited value in my own hand, I prefer to see the turn without inflating the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket deuces. The board is Ah-5c-3h-Tc. I am the pre-flop aggressor and checked the flop, and the opponent checked behind. On the turn, I have not improved my hand and have no showdown value against any Ace, Ten, or even a pocket pair higher than deuces. Given the passive nature of the action so far (both players checked the flop), checking again is the most prudent choice to keep the pot controlled and minimize further losses.

Flashlight has checked for the second time on a board of Ah-5c-3h-Tc. My Queen-high hand remains weak, but I still have a gutshot straight draw and have picked up a backdoor flush draw. By checking back, I realize my equity for free and avoid being blown off the hand by a potential check-raise. Flashlight's passive line suggests they may be holding a marginal hand or a weak Ace, and I prefer to see the river without inflating the pot.

The river has been checked around by both players, as indicated in the hand log, suggesting that the betting action for this hand is concluded. Given the current board (A-5-3-T-5) and my hand (2-2), I have two pair, which is a marginal hand that I have already checked. With no bets to call and no reason to bet, a check is the most appropriate and safe action.

The board is 5c-Ah-3h-Tc-5s. I hold Qc-4c, which is Queen-high and loses to any Ace, any pair, and King-high hands. Flashlight raised pre-flop from the Small Blind but has checked on every subsequent street (Flop, Turn, and River). This passive line usually indicates either a marginal hand that is afraid of the Ace (like a small pocket pair or a weak King) or a total air hand that has given up. While a bluff might occasionally work here, my Queen-high has a small amount of showdown value against missed lower straight or flush draws. In a tournament setting with a small pot, checking back to see the showdown and preserve my stack is a safer and more standard play than turning my hand into a bluff on a board where I have not represented much strength.

I am holding Ace-Queen of clubs, which is a premium starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Being on the button, I have the advantage of position. Raising to 300 is a standard opening bet to build the pot and apply pressure to the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 97o. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. 97o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against a raise. Calling would leave me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding, and 3-betting here is too speculative. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

With Ace-Ten suited in the Big Blind facing a standard button raise, we have a strong and highly playable hand. While 3-betting is certainly a viable option to take control, calling is a very standard play that keeps the opponent's range wide and allows us to see a flop with a hand that flops well.

I have top pair with a good kicker (Ace-Ten) on an Ace-high board with a flush draw. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position against the button raiser, standard strategy is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Flashlight to continuation bet with bluffs, draws, and weaker Aces, which we can then comfortably call. Donk betting would fold out their bluffs and only get action from stronger hands or draws.

I have top pair with a strong kicker on an Ace-high board. Since the opponent checked to me as the pre-flop raiser, I should continue with a value bet to gain information and extract value from weaker hands or pairs. The board has potential flush and straight draws, so betting also serves as protection.

We flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board with flush and straight draws. The button continuation bet 300 into a pot of 650. This is a clear call. Raising here would likely fold out their bluffs, weaker pairs, and draws while only getting action from stronger Aces (AJ+) or two-pairs/sets that beat us. By calling, we keep their range wide, including all their missed Broadway cards and various draws, while pot-controlling.

The turn brings a King, which connects well with the pre-flop raiser's range (like AK, KQ, KJ) but we still hold top pair with a Ten kicker. As the out-of-position defender, the standard line is to check again to the pre-flop aggressor to pot-control and allow them to continue bluffing or betting worse value hands. Leading out (donk betting) here is not advisable as it primarily gets called by stronger hands like AK, AQ, AJ, or two pairs, while folding out their bluffs.

I have top pair, top kicker on a board that is still relatively dry despite the King on the turn. Since I led on the flop and got called, I should continue to extract value from weaker Aces or middle pairs that may have called. The pot is 1250; a bet of 650 is a solid continuation that provides protection against potential draws while getting value from Pro's calling range.

The turn brings a King, which hits some of the pre-flop raiser's value range (like AK) but also introduces more semi-bluffs and pair+draw combos that we are currently beating. We hold top pair with a Ten kicker, which is too strong to fold against a half-pot bet given the numerous straight and flush draws available on this board. Calling pot-controls and keeps their bluffing range wide as we head to the river.

The river brings a 3, pairing the board and missing the flush draws. With AsTs, my best five-card hand is now two pair (Aces and Threes) with a King kicker from the board. This means my Ten kicker is counterfeited, and I will chop with any other Ace (like AQ or AJ) that doesn't make a better two pair (like AK or A4). Being out of position, the best line is to check. This pot-controls against stronger hands and allows Flashlight the opportunity to bluff their missed diamond or straight draws.

I am on the river with top pair, good kicker on an Ace-high board that paired the Trey. My opponent has called bets on the flop and turn, which suggests they likely have a King or a weaker Ace. Since the river did not complete any obvious draws (other than potentially random straights which are unlikely given the action), I believe my hand is strong enough to extract value from King-x or weak Ace hands that have been calling. Checking could win at showdown, but betting allows me to extract value from the opponent's calling range.

The river 3 pairs the board, counterfeiting our Ten kicker. Our best five-card hand is now Aces and Threes with a King kicker from the board. This means we chop with any other Ace that doesn't make a better two pair (like AQ, AJ, A9-A5). Flashlight bets 800 into a pot of 2550, which is roughly a 1/3 pot-sized bet. This small sizing gives us incredible pot odds (over 4:1) to call. Given the numerous missed diamond flush draws and straight draws (like 56s) in their range, along with the fact that we chop against many of their thin value bets (like AQ/AJ), folding is out of the question. We must call to catch bluffs and secure our share of the pot in chop scenarios.

Qd 7d is a standard opening hand from the button in 3-handed play. I will raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes in this tournament, to take the initiative and either win the blinds or play the hand in position against the small or big blind.

AQ offsuit is a premium holding. Facing a Button raise, 3-betting from the Small Blind is standard strategy to isolate the raiser, take the initiative, and deny the Big Blind the opportunity to enter the pot cheaply. I will 3-bet to 1000 to put pressure on the opener.

I hold Queen-Five offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very weak starting hand. With Flash (Button) opening and Pro (Small Blind) 3-betting, the action indicates significant strength from both opponents. Playing this weak hand out of position against a 3-bet is high-risk and negative expected value. Therefore, I will fold to conserve my stack.

I raised from the button with Qd 7d, a standard opening hand. However, Pro in the Small Blind has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. Q7 suited is too weak to call a 3-bet, even with position, as it is often dominated by Pro's 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, QJ, or pocket pairs). I will fold to preserve my stack and look for a better spot.
4d 8c is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. From the Button as the first to act, folding is the best decision to conserve my stack.

With the button folding, I am now heads-up against the Big Blind (Flash). Being in the small blind with 8h Td, I have a reasonable hand to attack the Big Blind's range. Raising to 250 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, given the stack sizes and the tendency for players to fold often.

I have KJo in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. The Small Blind has raised to 250. With a pot of 350 and only 150 more to call, I am getting excellent pot odds. Furthermore, I will have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand. Calling is the most prudent play here to see a flop and evaluate the strength of my hand while keeping the pot size controlled.

The flop of Ks-6c-3s is very dry but favors the Big Blind's calling range, which likely contains many Kings. My hand (8h Td) has completely missed, and attempting a continuation bet here would be a bluff with very little equity, as I would likely be called by any pair or better. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate if the opponent checks back or chooses to bet.

I have top pair with a King and a Jack kicker. Flashlight raised pre-flop and has now checked to me on a K-6-3 rainbow board (with two spades). This check often indicates either a weak hand, a mid-pair, or a trap with a very strong hand. By betting, I can extract value from weaker Kings (like K-T, K-9) and draws while protecting my hand against potential overcards or spade draws. I will bet 300 into the 500 pot.

The opponent has bet into me on a king-high board that favors their big blind calling range. Holding eight-high (8h-Td) with no significant equity or draw, I cannot continue profitably. Calling would be a bluff-catcher against a likely made hand, and raising would be an ill-advised bluff with no blockers. The most disciplined and correct strategic play is to fold and conserve my stack.

I am on the button with suited connectors (8d 7d), which is a strong hand to open 3-handed. Raising to 300 allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position.

I have pocket Queens, a premium starting hand. Flashlight raised from the button, which often indicates a wide range. I want to build the pot immediately and take control of the hand from out of position. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard size to discourage the Big Blind from calling and to extract value from Flashlight's button-opening range.
I have J5o in the Big Blind. There was an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. J5o is a trash hand and easily dominated. I need to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am facing a 3-bet from the small blind after opening with 8d 7d on the button. While 87s is a strong hand to open with, calling a 3-bet out of position is risky, but here I have the advantage of being on the button. The pot odds are favorable enough to justify a call to see a flop, as I can utilize my positional advantage and potential to hit straights, flushes, or high-equity draws to outplay my opponent post-flop.

I have an overpair with Pocket Queens on a relatively low, semi-coordinated board (Ts-9s-4h). I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws like flush draws or straight draws (e.g., J-Q, 8-7, J-8). A bet of approximately half the pot (1000 into 2100) is a standard continuation bet that keeps the initiative and extracts value from worse hands such as top pairs (Tx) or middle pairs (9x).

I hold 8d 7d on a board of Ts 9s 4h. This gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or J). The pot is 3100 and the bet is 1000. My pot odds are approximately 24% to call (1000 to win 4100), which is favorable given my outs. I should call to realize my equity and see the turn card, maintaining control of the pot size rather than raising and inflating the pot with a draw.

I hold pocket Queens on a board of Ts-9s-4h-2c. The 2nd of clubs on the turn is a brick that doesn't change the board's texture. I still have an overpair, and there are many hands in Flashlight's range that I currently beat, such as Jack-Ten, Ace-Ten, pocket Jacks, or various spade flush draws and straight draws (like QJ or J8). I need to continue betting for value and to protect my hand against these draws. A bet of 2200 (slightly more than half the pot) builds the pot for a potential river shove and forces Flashlight to pay to see the final card with their draws or marginal made hands.

With King-Jack offsuit in the small blind, I am facing a standard raise from the button. KJo is a reasonably strong hand that has good equity against a wide button opening range. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent holding while maintaining a controlled pot size, especially since the big blind has yet to act. While 3-betting is an option, I prefer to keep the pot manageable out of position unless I have a more premium hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh Jc. The Button (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 750. QJo is a strong enough broadway hand to play in a multi-way pot with these excellent pot odds (20% equity required). I will call to close the action pre-flop.

The flop of 7c 7s 9s is unfavorable for my King-Jack offsuit. As the first to act in a three-way pot, leading out with air (overcards with no pair or draw) is inefficient and unlikely to force folds from players who likely have pieces of this board or stronger hands. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the actions of my opponents.

In a multi-way pot on a paired board (7-7-9), I have two overcards (Qh, Jc) but no made hand. Flashlight has checked from the Small Blind. Given I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), checking is the most prudent play to see how the action develops and control the pot size.

The board of 7c 7s 9s favors the calling ranges of the blinds, which often include suited connectors, middle pocket pairs, and 9x hands. With two opponents checking to me, a continuation bet with King-Jack high and no spade draw is a low-equity bluff. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card and controls the size of the pot.

I have a spade in my hand (Js) giving me a flush draw on a board with three spades. The board is also paired, which increases the danger of someone having a full house or trips. I am out of position against two opponents who checked the flop. Leading out as a bluff is unlikely to be effective against two players and risks bloating the pot while I am behind. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and evaluate the river, or hopefully see a free showdown if it checks around.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades (Qs) improves my hand to top pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker. The board is now 7c-7s-9s-Qs. Flashlight has checked again. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) also checked the flop, there's a good chance neither opponent has a Seven or a very strong hand. While the flush draw (spades) is now live, I have a strong made hand and should bet for value and protection, rather than giving a free card. A bet of around half the pot is appropriate.

Facing a turn bet of 400 from the Big Blind after the flop checked through, I have the King of Spades giving me the second nut flush draw, along with a gutshot straight draw for a Ten. Given the pot odds and potential implied odds if I hit my flush or straight on the river, calling is mathematically sound and keeps my range wide enough to outplay my opponents on the river.

I am facing a bet of 400 into a pot that is already quite large. Holding the Jack of Spades on a board of 7c 7s 9s Qs, I have a flush draw to the Queen-high flush and a gutshot straight draw to a ten. Given the pot odds (roughly 4:1) and the potential for a large payout if I hit my flush on the river, calling is mathematically the correct decision. I am not closing the action if I call, but the price is too good to fold a draw with this much equity.

On the river, the board is A-Q-9-7-7. I hold King-Jack offsuit. I had a flush draw on the turn which missed on the river with the Ace of Clubs. My hand is now just King-high, which loses to any pair of Aces, Queens, Nines, or Sevens. Since both opponents have shown interest in the pot by calling/raising the turn, checking is the only logical play to minimize losses, as I have no showdown value and bluffing into two opponents is unlikely to succeed.

On the river, the Ace of Clubs is a significant scare card. My hand is currently two pair (Queens and Sevens with an Ace kicker). While I improved on the turn, the river Ace means any hand with an Ace now beats me (AA77x vs QQ77A). Furthermore, the flush draw (spades) completed on the turn. Since I was called by two opponents on the turn, the likelihood that my hand is still the best has decreased significantly. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and see if I can reach a cheap showdown. Betting here would likely only result in being called by hands that have me beaten, such as an Ace, a flush, or a seven.

We missed our straight and flush draws, leaving us with only King high. Both opponents showed strength on the turn with a bet and a call on a four-to-a-flush, paired board. It's highly likely one of them holds a made flush, a 7, or an Ace that just hit on the river. Bluffing into two opponents in this scenario is too risky and has a low success rate, so checking to concede the pot is the best play to preserve our stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. With a stack of 4925 and blinds at 50/100, I am the shortest stack and need to be aggressive to maintain competitiveness. While King-Deuce suited is a marginal hand, the button position allows me to attempt a steal of the blinds, which is a standard strategic move in a short-handed game to build my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Q6o. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. Q6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Against a standard Button opening range, I am significantly behind, and calling would also give the Big Blind (Pro) a good price to enter or squeeze. Given my strong chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal hand like this. I will fold.

9s7s is a suited one-gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop, making it a decent candidate to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. With pot odds of 200 to call for a total pot of 650 (about 31% equity needed), calling is mathematically sound and allows me to see a flop without unnecessarily bloating the pot out of position.

I flopped a gutshot straight draw and a backdoor flush draw, but I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. Checking to the raiser is standard here to evaluate their continuation bet sizing or take a free card if they check back.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a dry board of 8h-3s-Jd. My opponent checked to me. Although I have only King-high, a continuation bet is standard here to capitalize on my pre-flop lead and maintain the initiative, especially given my need to remain aggressive as the shortest stack at the table. A small bet should be sufficient to represent a hand like an Ace or a Jack.

Facing a near half-pot continuation bet out of position, my hand only has a gutshot straight draw and a backdoor flush draw. This doesn't provide enough direct pot equity to justify a call, as I only have about an 8.5% chance to hit the straight on the turn. Furthermore, being out of position limits my implied odds and maneuverability on future streets. Folding is the most mathematically sound and disciplined decision here.

I have been dealt pocket Kings (Kh Ks) on the button. This is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build a pot and extract value from my opponents. Throughout the tournament, my standard opening raise has been to 300 chips. I will maintain this consistency to avoid telegraphing the strength of my hand while still putting pressure on the blinds (Pro and Flashlight), both of whom have significantly smaller stacks than mine.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 3h, an extremely weak and unsuited hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, I have terrible absolute hand strength, no immediate equity, and would be playing out of position if I call. Folding is the only sensible play.

Flash is playing very aggressively from the button, raising frequently. I am in the big blind with 8c-5c, which is a playable suited connector hand. The pot odds (200 to call 450 total) make this a standard defensive call, as 8c-5c has reasonable potential to connect with the board or make a flush. I will call to see a flop.

The board texture of T-J-Q is extremely coordinated and hits the button's raising range significantly, as they likely hold high cards like broadway combinations. My hand (8c-5c) has very little equity here, with only a weak gutshot straight draw needing a nine. Being out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and re-evaluate based on their action.

I have pocket Kings (Kh Ks) on a board of Tc-Qd-Jh. This is a very coordinated board that hits the ranges of the blinds, particularly the Big Blind (Flashlight). The board offers many straight draws (any 9, any Ace, or combinations like K9, T9, etc.) and there are potential two-pairs or sets (QQ, JJ, TT). While I have an overpair and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts, checking back the flop allows me to control the pot size on a dangerous board where I could easily be behind or facing a check-raise. It also keeps my range protected and induces bluffs or thinner value bets on later streets if the board remains stable.

The board is currently Tc-Qd-Jh-6c. I hold 8c-5c, giving me a flush draw. The action on the turn was checked through by both myself and Flash. As I am in the Big Blind, I act first on the river. Since I have already checked through the turn, and I am in a position to see the river for free, I will check again to control the pot size and realize my equity.

I have an overpair (Kings) on a very coordinated board (Tc-Qd-Jh-6c). While the board is dangerous because many straight combinations (AK, K9, 98) and two-pair hands are possible, Flashlight has checked twice. This passive line from the Big Blind often indicates a medium-strength hand like a Queen or a Jack, or a draw that is looking for a cheap showdown. By checking back the flop, I have protected my range and controlled the pot. Now on the turn, I will bet for value to extract chips from those weaker pairs and draws. A bet of 400 in a 650 pot is a standard sizing that allows me to get called by hands I dominate while not overcommitting if I face a check-raise.

The board is Tc-Qd-Jh-6c. I hold 8c-5c, giving me a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw. Flash has raised to 400 into a 1050 pot. With my flush draw alone, I have significant equity to improve on the river. Considering the pot odds of 400 to win 1450 (approx 27.5%), and the potential for implied odds if I hit my flush or straight, calling is the mathematically sound play here to see the river.

The river card is the Ace of Clubs, completing my flush with 8c-5c. Given the Ace of Clubs is on the board, and I hold the 8c and 5c, I have an Ace-high flush, which is the current nuts. There is 1450 in the pot. I need to extract value from Flash, who has been aggressive. A bet of 750, roughly half the pot, is a solid value bet that could induce a call from a smaller flush, a straight, or even top pair/two pair hands given the action.

On the river, the board completes a Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T). Holding pocket Kings, I have the current nuts unless my opponent has two clubs for a flush. The board shows Tc, Qd, Jh, 6c, Ac, so a flush is a distinct possibility if Flashlight was chasing a draw. Flashlight's lead of 750 into the pot of 1450 is a strong action, but given that I have a King, it is highly likely that we are looking at a chop if Flashlight also holds a King (like KQ, KJ, or K9). Raising is risky because I will only be called by a flush that beats me or another King that results in a chop. Calling is the most prudent move to secure my portion of the pot while minimizing losses in case the opponent has successfully made a flush.
I am on the button with an unplayable offsuit gapper (8c 5d). This hand has poor equity and playability post-flop. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the small blind with 8d, Tc. Pro (the button) has folded, leaving only the big blind (Flash) to act after me. 8-10 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against a very aggressive big stack. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my remaining chips.

With Queen-high offsuit in the button position, I do not have a strong enough hand to contest the pot against the blinds, especially with a stack of 6725. Folding is the most disciplined and conservative play to preserve my chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s2h, which is a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. While I could potentially try to steal the blinds from Pro, 92o is bottom-tier and generally a fold even in small blind vs. big blind situations unless the opponent is exceptionally tight. Looking at previous hands, Pro has shown some resistance and competency. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7d 6h, which is a marginal hand. However, as the chip leader with over 150bb, I have been maintaining an aggressive pre-flop strategy throughout the tournament to pressure the smaller stacks. Raising from the button is a standard play to steal the blinds or take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size and should be sufficient to test the blinds' interest.

With pocket fives in the Small Blind facing a standard button open from Flash, we have a prime set-mining opportunity. The stack depths give us good implied odds if we hit our set on the flop. A 3-bet would bloat the pot out of position with a vulnerable hand, so flat-calling is the most profitable line.

I am in the big blind with J-6 offsuit, facing a raise from the button and a call from the small blind. The pot odds are very favorable (getting 200 to win 700, so 3.5:1), making this an easy call to close the action and see a flop.

I missed my set on a board with two overcards to my pocket fives in a multi-way pot. Being first to act out of position, checking is the standard play. I plan to re-evaluate based on the subsequent action, but will likely fold to any significant aggression.

I am in the big blind with J-6 offsuit on a board of 4-T-8. My hand has very little equity and no meaningful draws. Since the small blind has checked, betting into two opponents with only Jack-high is not a sound strategy, as it is unlikely to fold out better hands. Checking is the most prudent decision to see the turn for free, or to re-evaluate if the action continues.

I have an open-ended straight draw (6-7 on a 4-8-10 board). After raising pre-flop and being checked to by both opponents, a continuation bet is the best play. It exerts pressure on the smaller stacks and builds a pot for when I potentially hit my draw. A bet of around half the pot is standard to test their strength and potentially take the pot down immediately.

We missed our set on a board containing two overcards (Ten and Eight) to our pocket fives. Flash, the pre-flop raiser, fired a continuation bet of 500 into a pot of 900, and we still have Flashlight to act behind us. Our hand has very little equity against a likely stronger range and almost no playability out of position. Continuing here would be lighting chips on fire, so folding is the only logical decision.

I am holding Jack-high on a board of 4-10-8. The opponent has bet 500 into a pot of 1400. My hand has very little equity against a button-raising range, and there are no meaningful draws or made hands to justify a call. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack.

With A5o on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raise. A5o plays decently well as a blind steal, having an Ace blocker to the opponents' strong hands. Raising to 2.5x the big blind applies pressure without committing too many chips.

I have Ace-Eight suited, which is a solid starting hand in this position. Calling the 250 total allows me to see a flop with good equity and implied odds against the button's raise, without overcommitting out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 6s, a strong suited connector hand. The Button (Pro) raised to 2.5x the big blind, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I am being offered pot odds of 4-to-1 (calling 150 to win a total pot of 750), which requires roughly 20% equity. 7s 6s plays well in multi-way pots, and since I have the largest stack, I can comfortably call to see a flop and look for a straight or flush draw, or two-pair potential.

The board is dry and my Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw has low showdown value and limited equity against two opponents. Betting into this field is ill-advised as I would likely be called by any Queen or pair, effectively burning chips. Checking is the standard play here to control the pot size and observe the actions of my opponents, keeping my options open for later streets.

I have an open-ended straight draw with my 7s-6s on the Qd-4s-5c flop, needing either a 3 or an 8 to complete my hand. This gives me 8 outs. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked to me, and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is still left to act behind me. In a multi-way pot, checking is the most appropriate action with a draw, allowing me to see how the raiser acts and potentially close the action or see a turn card cheaply.

With middle pair and top kicker on a relatively dry board, checking behind in a 3-way pot is the safest and most standard option for pot control. Betting here might only get called by better hands like Qx or higher pocket pairs, while folding out weaker hands that I currently beat. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and can reassess my hand strength on the turn.

With the turn checking around, the pot remains small and my Ace-high hand has minimal showdown value. The board is paired with fours, and there are no strong draws for me. Given that I checked the flop and checked the turn so far, keeping the pot small and playing passively is the most disciplined approach to minimize potential losses while waiting for a better opportunity.

On the turn, the board pairs the 4 (Qd-4s-5c-4c). The flop was checked through by all three players, indicating that a strong hand like a Queen is unlikely to be held by anyone, or they are playing very cautiously. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (3s and 8s). Since Flashlight checked again, I will check as well. This allows me to potentially see a free river card to complete my straight or see if Pro (the pre-flop aggressor) decides to bet. If Pro bets a reasonable amount, I have the odds to call with my 8 outs; if it checks around, I get a free chance to improve.

Both opponents have checked to me twice on a Qd 4s 5c 4c board. My two pair (Fives and Fours with an Ace kicker) is very likely the best hand right now. Furthermore, holding the Ace of clubs gives me the nut club blocker, reducing the chances anyone is on the nut flush draw. Betting here gets value from weaker pairs or draws, denies free equity to overcards, and protects my hand.

The board is paired with a four and contains a Queen, making it highly likely that the button (Pro) has connected with the board or is value-betting a pocket pair. My Ace-high hand has minimal showdown value and no significant draws. Investing more chips into this pot, especially with another player (the Big Blind) still to act behind me, is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined choice to minimize losses.

On the turn, the board has paired the 4 (Qd-4s-5c-4c). Pro, the pre-flop aggressor who checked the flop, has now bet 350 into a 750 pot after Flashlight checked. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 3 or 8). To call 350, I am getting pot odds of approximately 3.14-to-1 (meaning I need about 24% equity). My direct equity to hit the straight on the river is about 17.4%. While I don't have the direct odds to call, the implied odds make up the difference. If I hit my straight, it will be well-disguised, and I can likely extract more than the 100-chip difference from Pro's remaining stack (6625). Additionally, my 7-high might occasionally be the best hand if Pro is bluffing with a smaller draw or air, though I am primarily calling for the draw. I will call to see the river.

On the river, the board is Qd-4s-5c-4c-Jd. I missed my open-ended straight draw (7s-6s) and have only 7-high, which has no showdown value. I am first to act against Pro (the button), who raised pre-flop and bet the turn after everyone checked the flop. Pro's turn bet was small (350 into 750), suggesting either a marginal made hand (like a 5 or a weak J) or a delayed continuation bet. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a 4 or a slow-played Queen, I currently hold a significant chip lead in the tournament. Risking a large portion of my stack on a bluff against a professional-level opponent who has shown interest in the pot is unnecessary. I will check and hope the hand goes to a cheap showdown or fold if faced with a significant bet.

On the river, the Jack of diamonds completes the board. My two pair (Fives and Fours) has some showdown value, but it's essentially third pair on this board. Flash called my turn bet, which means they could easily have a Queen, a better pocket pair that peeled, or a hand like a Jack that just improved. All worse hands, like busted club draws or straight draws, will fold to a bet. Since there are very few worse hands that will call and many better hands that will, betting here has no value. Checking behind to realize our showdown equity is the most optimal decision.

I am on the button with suited connectors (7d, Td). This is a strong enough hand to open from the button to steal the blinds or play in position. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and build the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Flashlight has opened for 300 from the Button. While Ace-Seven is a decent hand, I am out of position for the rest of the hand against Flashlight, who has been relatively active. However, in a 3-handed game, Ace-high hands are often ahead of a button opening range. Calling is an option, but I've been playing aggressively. Folding is too tight given the current blinds and stack sizes. A three-bet could put pressure on Flashlight, but with a stack of roughly 58 big blinds, I don't want to bloat a pot out of position with a mediocre Ace if I get 4-bet. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity, as Ace-Seven plays okay in a single-raised pot even out of position.

I am dealt Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak and uncoordinated hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, playing this junk hand out of position multi-way is extremely unprofitable. It's a clear fold.

On this flop of King-Ten-Five, the texture favors the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) who likely has many Kings, Tens, and straight draws in their range. My Ace-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable and doesn't warrant a lead. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. If they bet, I'll likely have to fold unless the bet is very small, as the board hits their opening range much harder than my calling range from the Small Blind.

I have middle pair with a Ten, which is decent but vulnerable on a King-high board. Flash checked to me, and since I am in position, checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting here might only get called by better hands like Kings or better Tens, while folding out hands that might try to bluff the turn. Checking back is the most balanced approach.

On the turn 9s, the board has become significantly more connected (Kd-Th-5h-9s). Flashlight's check on the flop suggests they might not have a strong King or they are playing a medium-strength hand like a Ten or a pocket pair cautiously. My Ace-high (A7o) still has some showdown value against Flashlight's potential air or missed draws, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. Betting as a bluff is also risky because the board texture hits many hands in a Button opening range that Flashlight might have checked on the flop (like J-T, Q-T, or 9-8). I will check to see if Flashlight checks behind again, which would allow me to reach the river and potentially win at showdown without committing more chips.

I have middle pair with a Ten, which is decent but vulnerable on a King-high board. Flash checked to me, and since I am in position, checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting here might only get called by better hands like Kings or better Tens, while folding out hands that might try to bluff the turn. Checking back is the most balanced approach.

On the river, the Ace of Clubs (Ac) pairs my hole card. Given that both Flashlight and I checked the flop and turn, it's unlikely either of us holds a King or a Ten, or at least a strong one. By checking the river, I am hoping to induce a bluff from Flashlight if they have a missed heart draw or a straight draw (like QJ or 87). If Flashlight has a mediocre hand like a pocket pair or a Ten, they might check behind, but they likely would have folded those to a lead anyway. If they bet, I have a very strong bluff-catcher/value hand with top pair.

The board shows an Ace and a King, which makes my pair of Tens extremely vulnerable. Flash checked to me on the river, suggesting either a marginal hand or a give-up. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as most would have folded or checked earlier, and it would likely only be called by better hands like an Ace or King. Therefore, checking behind is the most prudent action to reach showdown and minimize potential losses.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin over both opponents. Holding 4-3 offsuit on the button, this is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While opening from the button in a three-handed game is generally encouraged to steal the blinds, 4-3o is at the bottom of the range and does not play well post-flop if called. Given my stack advantage, I prefer to wait for better spots to apply pressure rather than risking chips with air.

With A6o in the small blind and it folded to me, I have a decent Ace that is often ahead of the big blind's random hand. Raising to 3x (300) puts pressure on the BB, taking the initiative and potentially winning the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 3s 7h, a very weak hand. My opponent (Pro, in the Small Blind) raised to 300. With such a low-equity holding and being out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no strategic justification to call or raise. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.
A8o is a strong enough starting hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to try and steal the blinds or play heads-up in position.

Holding 5d 3c in the small blind against a button raise is extremely weak. There is no strategic value in defending this hand out of position. Calling would be a losing play in the long run as I will likely miss the flop and be forced to fold or commit more chips to a hand with no equity. The best course of action is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

Pro has raised to 2.5x from the button, and I am in the big blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. While the pot odds are tempting (150 to call into a pot of 400), Queen-Seven is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position. Pro has shown a tendency to open wide from the button, but playing a dominated or disconnected hand like this often leads to difficult post-flop situations. Given my significant chip lead, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot rather than bleeding chips in small pots out of position.

I am on the button with 6s 9d, which is a weak hand with poor playability. Given the strength of the hand and the position, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term strategy, avoiding unnecessary risk against the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-5 offsuit, a very weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, I am currently the chip leader and don't need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands, especially when I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand if called. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

In a 3-handed game, any King-high hand is generally strong enough to open from the button. Raising to 300 allows me to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. With my significant chip lead, maintaining aggression in position is a key strategy to keep my opponents' stacks under pressure.

I am dealt an extremely weak hand (84 offsuit) in the small blind. The button has already opened with a raise to 300. Playing such a weak hand out of position is a losing play, so folding is the only viable option.

Facing a standard button raise, my Big Blind defense with A2o is mathematically sound given the pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450). I will call to see a flop and evaluate from there.

I am in the Big Blind and first to act on the flop. The board [Js, Kd, Tc] is highly coordinated and heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). Since I have no pair and no immediate betting initiative, checking is the correct fundamental play to see how the button proceeds.

I have top pair with Kings on a highly coordinated board (Js-Kd-Tc). While my kicker (3d) is weak, checking back gives too much equity to draws and allows the opponent to potentially bluff later. A bet of 300 into the 650 pot allows me to extract value from weaker pairs like Jacks or Tens and charge various straight draws containing an Ace, Queen, or Nine. If I face significant resistance, I can slow down on later streets, but for now, a continuation bet is the standard and most profitable play.

The board [Js, Kd, Tc] is highly coordinated and connects strongly with the button's opening range, which often contains Broadways and suited connectors.
