Ten-Three suited is a very weak hand with low equity. Given that I'm first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game and both blinds are yet to act, folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, Jack-Deuce offsuit. Given the position and hand strength, there is no strategic value in investing more chips to see a flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve my stack.

I am in the Button position with K2o. While stealing the blinds is a valid strategy, K2o is a very weak hand to play with, especially against two opponents. With 53 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a marginal holding. I will fold.

I have 9c Tc in the Small Blind. After the Button folds, it's a standard situation to raise into the Big Blind with a strong suited connector. A raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on Pro, while giving me control of the hand if they call.

Flash is raising to 3x from the small blind. While we have position, K7o is a marginal offsuit hand that suffers from poor playability post-flop and can often be dominated. Against a 3x open, folding is the most standard and solid choice.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, 6-deuce suited. While I have a significant chip lead and have been opening frequently from the button to steal the blinds, this hand is at the very bottom of my range. Continuing to raise every button can make me predictable and encourage my opponents to 3-bet or defend more aggressively. I will fold this junk hand to maintain a more balanced image and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

JTo is a solid hand to open from the small blind when it is folded to us. By raising to 3x the big blind, I put pressure on the big blind to fold weak hands and can often take down the pot pre-flop. Even if called, JTo plays reasonably well post-flop.

I am in the big blind with Jack-seven offsuit, which is a weak and uncoordinated hand. Although the pot odds are tempting, it is generally best to fold weak hands out of position rather than chasing a small pot, especially against a raise from the small blind.
T5o is a very weak offsuit hand, not suitable for a Button steal against the current blind stacks. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, making this a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. I hold Queen-Seven suited, which is a reasonable hand to play in this spot. Completing the small blind (calling the 50 chips) to see a flop is a standard, low-risk strategic choice given the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 4h. The Button folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just called to complete. I have a very weak hand, but since the action is on me and I can see a flop for free, checking is the most logical play. There is no reason to bloat the pot with a marginal holding, and I will have position on Flashlight for the rest of the hand.

I hold top pair on a dry, rainbow board. Betting half the pot (100 into 200) is a standard value bet that allows me to protect my hand against potential draws or weaker pairs while gathering information about the opponent's strength in this heads-up scenario.

In this blind-versus-blind situation, I have bottom pair (fours) on a relatively dry board. Flashlight completed the small blind and is leading for a half-pot bet of 100. Given the wide ranges involved in SB vs BB battles and the favorable pot odds (3-to-1), folding bottom pair is too tight. I am in position and can evaluate the turn based on Flashlight's next action. Raising here might overplay a marginal hand, so calling to see a turn is the most balanced and effective play.

I am at the turn holding top pair with a Queen. The turn card (5c) did not complete any obvious draws, though it adds some straight possibilities. Both players checked the turn in the previous log, but since I am effectively in control of my action, I will check to control the pot size, given that my kicker is weak and I don't want to bloat the pot against a potentially stronger hand or a drawing hand that has now picked up equity.

On this turn, I still hold bottom pair (fours). Flashlight led the flop but has now checked the turn. This check often indicates either a marginal hand looking for a cheap showdown or a total air hand that has given up. While I could bet to try and push Flashlight off a slightly better hand like a weak Queen or a small pocket pair, my hand has some showdown value and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3). Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and keep the pot small, which is ideal with a marginal holding in position.

I hold top pair (Queens) with a weak 7 kicker. Given that both players checked the turn and the river card (Ten) does not complete any obvious draws, my hand is a marginal value hand. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands (e.g., a better Queen, two pair, or a slow-played set), while checking back allows me to reach showdown safely. Since the pot is relatively small and I am in a heads-up pot, checking is the most prudent strategy to avoid losing unnecessary chips.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board of Q-4-2-5-T. Flashlight limped from the small blind, bet the flop, and then checked both the turn and the river. This sequence often indicates a weak made hand or a missed draw. While a pair of fours is not strong, it has significant showdown value in a small, limped pot against an opponent who has shown weakness on the later streets. Betting is unlikely to get a better hand to fold, nor is it likely to be called by a worse hand. Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent and profitable play.

With 9-7 offsuit, I have a very weak starting hand with poor equity against any calling range. Calling 100 chips into a 150-chip pot is mathematically unsound as the hand performs poorly in multi-way pots. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

In the Small Blind with T6o, I am facing a fold from the button. I am getting 3:1 odds to complete the bet, but T6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a competent opponent (Pro) in the Big Blind. While I have a large chip lead, folding is the most disciplined and lowest-variance play here, as raising or calling puts me in a marginal spot post-flop out of position.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is very healthy at over 16,000 chips. Opening for a 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent strategy throughout the tournament to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. Both opponents have folded or called wide in the past, so this raise should either win the blinds or allow me to play a pot in position with an Ace-high hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K8o, facing a button raise from Flash. K8o is too weak to play out of position against a raise, and there is still the Big Blind left to act. The clear decision is to fold.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (9-3 offsuit). The pot odds do not justify calling a raise of 300 from the button, especially since I am out of position. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the Button with Eight-Deuce offsuit, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this holding. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I am in the Small Blind position with 7s9h after the button (Pro) folded. The pot is currently 150 (50 from me, 100 from the big blind). It costs me 50 to call and see a flop. While 7-9 offsuit is a weak hand, the pot odds are favorable for a blind-versus-blind situation, and completing the small blind is a standard play here to see a flop cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d5d, a speculative suited hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the blind. I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to try and take the pot down immediately. Given Flashlight's short stack and the fact that I have position post-flop, checking is the most prudent and low-risk play to see if I hit a strong draw or pair on the flop without bloating the pot with a weak starting hand.

I am in the small blind with 7-high on a K-10-6 rainbow board. I have missed the flop entirely and have no significant equity. Betting here would be a pure bluff into the Big Blind, who likely has a range that connects reasonably well with King-high boards. Checking is the standard, disciplined play to control the pot size and avoid putting more chips in with a weak, non-showdown value hand.

On a flop of Td Kh 6d, I have a backdoor flush draw with my 3d 5d. Flashlight (Small Blind) checked to me. While I could attempt to bluff, the pot is small (200) and the board hits a lot of a completing range (Kings, Tens). Checking back is the most sensible play to see a free turn card and potentially improve my equity without risking chips on a pure air hand.

I am in the Small Blind position with 7s9h on a Td Kh 6d Qh board. I have no pair and no realistic drawing equity. As I have already checked on the turn, I am simply playing to realize my hand's value, which is effectively none. I will continue to check-fold against any aggression from the Big Blind, as this board is highly favorable to their range.

On the turn, the board is now Td Kh 6d Qh. I have a flush draw (3d 5d with Td 6d on board). The Queen of Hearts is a card that likely hits many of the hands Flashlight would limp with, but they have checked twice now (flop and turn). I have zero showdown value currently but significant equity with the flush draw. Since Flashlight has shown weakness by checking again, I can take a stab at the pot. However, given the small pot size and my position, checking back to take a free river card and realize my equity is a lower-variance play. If I hit my flush, I can value bet; if I miss, I can decide whether to bluff based on the river card.

The board shows Td-Kh-6d-Qh-Js, which means there is a straight on the board (T-J-Q-K). My hand, holding a 9, gives me a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). Since it has been checked down to the river, I believe my hand has significant showdown value. An Ace would make an Ace-high straight (A-K-Q-J-T), but it is unlikely the opponent has an Ace if they have checked every street. I will make a value bet of 100 to extract some chips from any weaker made hands like two pair or sets that may feel compelled to call.

The board is Td-Kh-6d-Qh-Js, which is highly coordinated. Any Ace or Nine makes a straight. I have 3d-5d, which is 5-high with no showdown value, as I missed my flush draw. Flashlight has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. Even if they are bluffing, I cannot call with 5-high, as I lose to virtually every other hand in their range. Attempting a raise-bluff on such a board is extremely risky because Flashlight could easily have hit any part of this straight-heavy texture. Folding is the most disciplined and low-risk option.

I am in the button position with 7s 4s, which is a weak speculative hand. Playing this hand from the button without any previous action is not profitable given the lack of equity and the necessity to play tight in a 150-hand tournament. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. While it is folded to me, this hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially against a solid opponent in the Big Blind. I will fold and look for better opportunities to use my chip lead.

I am on the button with 8d 2h, which is a very weak starting hand. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, 8-2 offsuit does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open raise or a call, especially since I want to protect my lead as the table chip leader. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

In a Small Blind vs. Big Blind situation, 98o is a marginal offsuit connector. While it can sometimes be limped or raised as a steal, folding is a very safe and standard play to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop.
On the button in a 3-handed game, A4o is strong enough to open-raise and try to steal the blinds. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to put pressure on the SB and BB.

Holding 3s-6h in the small blind is a very weak hand with poor potential for post-flop equity. Being out of position against the button, defending this hand is negative expected value. Folding is the correct discipline to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 5c, a medium-strength suited connector. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 400. This gives me pot odds of roughly 2.6 to 1. Given our deep stacks and the fact that my hand has decent post-flop playability (straight and flush potential), this is a standard defend in the Big Blind against a button raise. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 5c. The flop is 4c As Ts, giving me a backdoor flush draw and a very marginal backdoor straight draw. The current pot is 550 and it is my turn to act first. Against Pro, who raised from the Button, this board favors his range significantly as it contains an Ace and a Ten. Given that I have almost no direct equity and only weak backdoor potential, I will check and see how the Button responds. If he bets, I will likely fold unless the bet is extremely small, as this board hits a standard Button raising range very hard.

I flopped top and bottom pair on a draw-heavy board with two spades and broadway cards. Flash checked to me, so I should bet for value and to protect my equity against flush and straight draws. Betting around half to two-thirds of the pot will extract value from weaker Aces, Tens, and drawing hands while charging them appropriately.

On a flop of As Ts 4c, my hand (8c 5c) has very little equity. I have no pair and only a marginal backdoor flush draw. This board significantly favors Pro's range, as he raised from the Button and likely has many Aces, Tens, and spade draws. Pro's bet of 300 into 550 is a standard continuation bet on a board that hits him well. Given that I have almost no way to win the hand without a massive bluff or hitting a runner-runner sequence, the best move is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with 5-high offsuit (5h, 4c), which is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. With about 55 big blinds, there is no reason to play this hand aggressively or limp-call. Folding is the standard, disciplined approach.

I am in the Small Blind with J3s. The Button (Flashlight) has folded. I need to put in 50 more to complete the 100 Big Blind or I can raise to steal the pot. Pro is in the Big Blind and has shown a tendency to play somewhat tight, but also defends appropriately. J3s is a weak hand, but in a blind-vs-blind situation, it has some value. However, completing or raising into Pro with such a weak hand out of position for the rest of the hand is often suboptimal unless I have a specific read. Given the current stack dynamics and hand history, I will fold this hand to avoid playing a weak hand out of position against a competent opponent.

7c 3d is a very weak hand, even from the button in a three-handed game. While I have been aggressive in stealing blinds throughout the tournament, this specific hand has almost no post-flop playability or equity. Given my significant chip lead, I can afford to wait for a slightly better hand to apply pressure.

With a middle pocket pair (66) in the small blind and the button folded, raising is the best play to deny the big blind a free realization of their equity. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and allows us to take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Given that I have position for the rest of the hand and A9 is a strong enough hand to defend against a Small Blind open, calling is the most balanced and disciplined play. Raising could force me to commit to a larger pot with a hand that might be dominated or vulnerable if I miss, so I will see a flop and evaluate based on the texture.

With an underpair to the Ace and Jack on the flop, my hand has very little equity against my opponent's continuing range. While this board strongly favors my pre-flop raising range, a c-bet with 66 would strictly be a bluff. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially see a free turn card, and easily fold to a bet if my opponent connects with this board.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board, although there is a flush draw present. Pro checked to me, which often indicates a trap or a weak range. Given I have the best hand currently, I should bet to charge potential draws for the next card and gain value from worse Aces or middle/bottom pairs that Pro might have continued with pre-flop. Betting half the pot (300) is a balanced sizing to accomplish this.

My opponent bet into me on an Ace-Jack high board after I checked. My pocket sixes have virtually no equity against their betting range here. Continuing would mean calling with a weak underpair out of position, which is a losing play. Folding is the safest and most optimal decision.
83o is a very weak hand, especially when facing full action behind me. I will fold.

I am in the small blind with A4o, a hand with decent equity to see a flop against the big blind after the button has folded. Completing the small blind is a low-risk strategy to play this hand and attempt to outplay the opponent post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o. The Button (Pro) has folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the blind. I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to try and take the pot down immediately. Flashlight's stack is quite short (56.75 BBs), and my hand has decent high-card value. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and see if I hit a King or a favorable board, as K7o doesn't particularly want to play a bloated pot out of position if I get raised back or called and miss. I will check.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on an Ace-high board. Given the dry texture of the board (A-9-2), betting into my opponent could alienate weaker hands while only getting called by better Aces. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control, keep the pot small, and see how the hand develops on the turn without committing unnecessary chips.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high. The board is Ace-high and the pot was limped pre-flop. Flashlight (Small Blind) checked to me on the flop. While I could attempt a bluff, King-high has some marginal showdown value against a total air range from the Small Blind in a limped pot. There is no need to inflate the pot with a weak hand on a board that likely connects with an Ace or draws. I will check and take a free turn card.

With top pair and a dry board, checking through the flop and turn has been a cautious approach. However, the board is relatively static and my Ace is likely the best hand given the lack of aggression from the big blind. I will place a small probe bet of 100 into the 200 pot to extract value from any weaker pair or to potentially take down the pot now.

In this limped pot, the board has an Ace and a Nine. Flashlight led for 100 into 200 on the turn after the flop was checked around. While King-high has some theoretical showdown value in a limped pot, it is unlikely to be the best hand here given the lead on an Ace-high board. I have no draws and only one overcard to the nine. Since I have a significant chip lead and it is a small pot, there is no reason to make a marginal call with a weak hand. I will fold.

I am holding Jack-high offsuit (Jack of Diamonds, Eight of Clubs) on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a weak starting hand that does not warrant an investment of chips, especially given the stack sizes and the multi-way dynamic. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I have a pocket pair (44) in the Small Blind. Since it has been folded to me, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind (Pro). Pocket pairs are quite strong in short-handed play, especially blind vs blind. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and attempt to win the pot immediately or narrow Pro's calling range.

I am in the Big Blind with T3s facing a 3bb raise from the Small Blind. T3s is a very weak hand with poor playability, and calling or raising here is a long-term losing play. The best action is to fold.

I am on the button with T5o. In a 3-handed game, T5o is generally considered too weak to open raise from the button, especially when both opponents are relatively active. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, the hand has very poor playability if called. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

With action folded to me in the Small Blind, K8o is a perfectly viable hand to steal the Big Blind's relatively short stack. Raising to 3 chips (300 total) puts pressure on Flashlight to defend with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (6h 4d). While the pot odds offer a 2:1 return, my hand has very little equity against a Small Blind raise range. Defending the big blind with such a marginal hand leads to difficult post-flop situations where I will often be dominated or outkicked. Given the stack depths and the strength of my cards, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision.
AJ offsuit on the Button is a strong starting hand. Raising to 2.5x or 3x the big blind is standard to thin the field and take control of the pot. I will raise to 250.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (5h, 4d). Calling a raise from the button out of position with such low-value cards is mathematically unfavorable and risky, especially since the big blind has yet to act. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 3h, which is one of the weakest possible hands. The player 'Pro' on the Button has raised to 2.5x (250). To call, I would need to put in 150 more. Although the odds are decent, 23 offsuit plays terribly post-flop and has almost zero equity against a standard Button opening range. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead as the tournament progresses toward the final hands.

I am on the button with Ace-King suited, a premium starting hand. I am first to act pre-flop. Raising is the standard play to build the pot and take the initiative against the blinds. A raise to 300 is consistent with my typical opening size in this tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard late-position steal attempt. Ace-high holds significant equity against a button opening range, which usually includes many weak kings, queens, and suited connectors. However, A3o is not a very strong hand to play out of position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the lead. I could consider a 3-bet (raise) to take the pot down immediately, but Flashlight has a relatively short stack (5425) compared to the blinds, and I don't want to bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal Ace. Calling 250 more into a pot of 450 is mathematically acceptable (getting nearly 3:1), but given the tournament strategy to maximize the stack and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding or calling are both viable. Since I have a comfortable chip lead, I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as an Ace is often the best hand pre-flop against a wide button range.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 6d, an unsuited and disconnected weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, the pot odds are not good enough to justify entering the pot with this marginal holding. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw on a dry Queen-high board. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they respond. If they check back, I maintain equity; if they bet, I will evaluate the price for my overcard and straight draw.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-King. The flop is relatively dry with no immediate draws other than the backdoor potential for my diamonds. My opponent has checked to me. A continuation bet is standard here to capitalize on my equity and represent the Queen I may have connected with, or simply to take the pot down now if the opponent is weak. A half-pot bet of 350 is appropriate to test the waters.

On a Queen-high flop (Qh-4s-2d), I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Flashlight has bet 350 into a pot of 700, giving me 3:1 odds. To call profitably based on pot odds alone, I would need roughly 25% equity. With only 4 outs for the straight and 3 potential outs for the Ace (which might not even be good if the opponent has a better Ace or a Queen), my immediate equity is approximately 15% to hit on the turn. Since I am out of position and the hand is marginal, the best strategy to protect my lead and maximize my stack is to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I have been dealt Ace-King of hearts on the button, which is a premium starting hand in three-handed play. My goal is to build the pot and capitalize on my positional advantage. Throughout the tournament, I have consistently used a 3x opening raise (300 chips). I will maintain this sizing to remain balanced while exerting pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (95o). Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, I have terrible equity and will be out of position post-flop. This is an easy and standard fold.

I am holding 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While I am in the big blind and have received a discount to call, the pot odds do not justify calling with such a dominated hand against a button raise. Playing out of position with this range will lead to poor results, and it is better to fold and conserve my stack.
82s is a very weak hand, even suited. On the button facing the blinds, this hand does not have enough playability or equity to justify opening. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (9c-6s). With 9c-6s being very low-equity and playing out of position against a large stack (Flash), the most prudent strategic decision is to fold and conserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Given my stack size and position, I need to take the initiative. A standard raise is the best way to leverage my hand strength, potentially take down the blinds immediately, or play a post-flop hand with the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand to defend against a raise from the Button. Given that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand and the Big Blind is a strong player yet to act, folding is the standard and most prudent play to maintain my chip lead.

With pocket 3s in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x raise from the Button, I have a clear set-mining opportunity. Both my opponent and I have enough chips to give me the implied odds needed to profitably call 200 chips in hopes of hitting a set on the flop.

The flop of 8-8-9 with two clubs is highly connected and hits a significant portion of the Button's pre-flop raising range. My pocket 3s represent a very weak underpair with almost no chance to improve. Being out of position, the standard and safest play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If faced with a bet, my hand is an easy fold.

I am heads-up against the Big Blind on a paired board of 8-8-9. My hand, Ace-Queen high, has little immediate equity besides backdoors and overcards, but this is a classic continuation bet spot. Since the Big Blind checked to me, betting approximately half the pot (300 chips) is the standard move to represent strength, potentially forcing a fold from the opponent's weaker holdings or missed high cards.

On a flop of 8-8-9 with two clubs, my pocket 3s are a very weak underpair. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many overcards, flush draws, and even 8x or 9x hands. Facing a continuation bet of 300, my hand has virtually no chance of improving and is easily beaten. Folding is the only sensible option.

I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit. In a three-handed game, the button opening range should be quite wide. King-high hands have significant equity against random blind hands. I will make a standard raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and capitalize on my positional advantage and chip lead. If I face significant resistance (a large 3-bet), the hand is weak enough to fold comfortably.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand, Ten-Four. The Button has opened with a raise to 300. Calling or 3-betting from out of position with this holding is highly unprofitable. Folding is the only correct decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, Jack-Deuce offsuit. Flash has raised from the button, and Pro has folded. Calling 200 to win a total pot of 400 is not profitable with this holding, and being out of position against an aggressive button makes post-flop play difficult. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

74o is a very weak starting hand. From the button, attempting to steal with such a poor holding is unprofitable, especially with two players still to act. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

My hand [2s, Td] is very weak, disconnected, and has poor playability. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, I prefer to fold this hand to conserve my stack and avoid playing a disadvantaged hand out of position against Flash.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, is a reasonable opening hand for this position, allowing me to potentially steal the blinds and take control of the pot. Given the tournament context and my stack size, raising to 300 is a standard and aggressive play to test the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised from the Button to 300. Since I would be playing out of position with a marginal hand and I have a significant stack lead, there is no reason to call or raise. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

With King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Button open, I have a strong enough hand to defend. Calling is a solid choice to see a flop against a wide Button range, keeping the pot manageable and retaining the opponent's weaker hands that I dominate.

I flopped top pair with a very strong King kicker. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most balanced play is to check to the raiser to see if they continuation bet. This gives me the opportunity to evaluate their action and potentially check-raise or call depending on the sizing.

I have Queen-high on a board of Jack-Seven-Trey. Since I am on the button and my opponent checked, I have the option to take a free card. Given my lack of a made hand or strong draw, betting into a checked board with high-card strength is less optimal than checking behind to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings an Ace, which is an overcard to our pair of Jacks and hits a significant portion of our opponent's pre-flop raising range that might have checked back the flop (like AK, AQ, AT, Axs). Since betting now is unlikely to get called by many worse hands and will definitely get called or raised by an Ace, checking for pot control is the best option. We can evaluate their action and decide whether to call a bet or see a free river.

The turn Ace is a significant card that likely connects with the big blind's calling range. My Queen-high is weak and essentially a bluff catcher. Betting here would likely be called by any Ace or Jack, and would serve no value. Checking behind is the most prudent play to control the pot size and realize equity for free on the river.

On the river, the board completes a possible diamond flush and there is an Ace on the board. I have second pair with a good kicker (Jacks with a King). Since Flashlight checked both the flop and the turn, their range is likely capped or composed of marginal hands, missed draws, or possibly a trapped flush. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like 88-TT or King high) but will certainly be called by better hands (like an Ace or a flush). Checking allows us to see a free showdown and potentially catch a bluff if Flashlight decides to stab at the pot with a missed draw.

I have Queen-high on a board where the flush draw completed on the river. Pro checked the river. My hand has zero value, and betting would only be a bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any calling range. Checking behind to reach showdown is the only logical decision.

I am holding pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am on the button and have a significant chip lead. Throughout the tournament, I have consistently opened for 300 chips from late positions. To maintain consistency with my betting patterns and to build a pot with a premium hand while inviting action from the blinds, I will raise to 300.
4s 5d is a very weak offsuit starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button while out of position in the Small Blind, the standard and most optimal play is to fold to preserve my stack.

Flash is raising from the button, and I am in the big blind. While Q6o is not a premium hand, the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 650) make this a standard defensive call. Folding would be too passive, and raising risks inflating a pot with a marginal holding against an aggressive opponent. I will call and evaluate the flop.

The flop [Jc, 6c, Ac] is highly uncoordinated with my pair of 6s and extremely dangerous due to the two clubs and the high cards (Ace, Jack) which heavily favor the button's opening range. I have no flush draw and my pair is likely behind any Ace, Jack, or strong club draw. Checking is the standard play to preserve my stack and see how the aggressor proceeds without investing more chips into a likely losing hand.
